title: Cutting a mortise and tenon joint
subtitle: Mark, saw, and chop the standard through-tenon joint for frame joinery
excerpt: How to lay out and cut a through mortise and tenon joint: setting the mortise gauge, sawing the tenon cheeks and shoulders, chopping the mortise from both faces, and testing fit before gluing.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (1880s). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Traditional Building Skills syllabus (SPAB).

glossaryTerms.definition: The rectangular slot cut into one timber to receive the tenon of another. A through mortise passes completely through the timber; a stopped (blind) mortise stops short of the far face.
The two long flat faces of a tenon, sawn parallel to the timber face. The cheeks provide the gluing and bearing surfaces that give the joint its strength.

The flat cross-grain face at the base of each cheek, cut perpendicular to the timber length. The shoulder sits tight against the face of the mortised timber and hides any slight sloppiness in the cheek fit.

content.text: The mortise and tenon is the core structural joint in frame joinery. A well-fitted joint has two long gluing faces (the
tenon cheeks
), two tight
tenon shoulders
, and a
mortise
that is a push-fit on the tenon, not a sliding fit. A joint that rattles before gluing will crack at the shoulder line under load.
Proportions

The standard proportions for a through mortise and tenon: the tenon thickness is one third of the timber thickness (for 45 mm (1 3/4 in) timber, the tenon is 15 mm (9/16 in) thick). The mortise width matches the chisel width closest to that dimension. The tenon width is equal to the timber depth minus 10 mm (3/8 in) on each side. These proportions give a joint with maximum glue area that is still practical to chop cleanly.
Step 1, mark up the tenon

Set a marking gauge to one third of the timber thickness and scribe both cheek lines around all four faces of the tenon end. Set a second gauge (or reset the first) to mark the shoulder depth, typically equal to the depth of the mortised timber. Run the shoulder line across all four faces with a sharp marking knife against a try square. Mark the waste areas with a pencil cross.
Step 2, saw the cheeks

Grip the timber at 45 degrees in a vice or cramp. Place the tenon saw on the waste side of the cheek line (a saw kerf placed on the line removes the line and narrows the tenon). Start the cut with two or three backwards strokes to establish the kerf, then saw diagonally down to the shoulder line, watching the far edge of the timber. Bring the saw to horizontal and complete to the shoulder. Repeat for the second cheek. The saw should never cross the shoulder line.
Step 3, saw the shoulders

Lay the timber flat and cut both shoulders with a tenon saw against the knife line. Keep the saw on the waste side. The shoulder cut meets the cheek cut precisely at the corner; any rounding at this corner leaves a visible gap at the face of the joint.
Step 4, chop the mortise

Mark the mortise on the timber face using the tenon itself as a template. Secure the timber in a vice. With a chisel matching the mortise width, start 2 mm inside the end line and chop downward with the mallet to half the depth. Remove waste by levering horizontally. Turn the timber and chop from the second face to meet the first pass. Clean the end walls last, chopping squarely to the marked lines. The mortise walls must be flat and parallel; a bowed wall gives a rocking fit.
Step 5, test and adjust

Assemble dry. The tenon should push in with hand pressure and hold without a cramp. Both shoulders should seat flush against the mortised face. If the joint is tight, identify whether it is the cheeks or the end walls binding: chalk the cheeks lightly, push the joint in, and the chalk marks on the mortise walls show the high spots. Pare those spots only; do not open the joint evenly.
Common mistakes
Sawing on the line rather than the waste side.
A saw kerf is 2 to 3 mm wide. If the saw straddles the line, the cheek is undersize and the joint is sloppy before gluing.
Chopping the mortise to full depth in one pass.
Chopping too deep in one pass levers the side walls off-line. Work in progressive passes of 5 to 6 mm depth, removing waste before chopping deeper.
Using a chisel wider than the mortise.
A chisel wider than the mortise has no control over where the bevel cuts. Match chisel width to the mortise width exactly.
